Vue/Nuxt Project Architecture: How to Pick the Right Folder Structure for Your Team
Vue/Nuxt project architecture: how to pick the right folder structure for your team

A project's folder structure is actually really important. Why? Because when it comes to managing and maintaining the project, a good structure tells you where things belong and where to find them. Pair that with a clear README.md, and other devs can pick up your project fast. This is also kind of an indirect way to keep code quality in check.
First up, here's a basic Vue project structure. I'll use a project scaffolded by
as the example, so here are the relevant details:
You should see something like this:

vue-example/
├── .vscode/                    # VSCode editor settings
│   ├── extensions.json
│   └── settings.json
├── e2e/                        # E2E tests (Playwright)
│   ├── tsconfig.json
│   └── vue.spec.ts
├── public/                     # static assets
│   └── favicon.ico
├── src/                        # source code directory
│   ├── assets/                 # asset files
│   │   ├── base.css
│   │   ├── logo.svg
│   │   └── main.css
│   ├── components/             # Vue components
│   │   ├── __tests__/          # component tests
│   │   │   └── HelloWorld.spec.ts
│   │   ├── icons/              # icon components
│   │   │   ├── IconCommunity.vue
│   │   │   ├── IconDocumentation.vue
│   │   │   ├── IconEcosystem.vue
│   │   │   ├── IconSupport.vue
│   │   │   └── IconTooling.vue
│   │   ├── HelloWorld.vue
│   │   ├── TheWelcome.vue
│   │   └── WelcomeItem.vue
│   ├── router/                 # Vue Router config
│   │   └── index.ts
│   ├── stores/                 # Pinia state management
│   │   └── counter.ts
│   ├── views/                  # page components
│   │   ├── AboutView.vue
│   │   └── HomeView.vue
│   ├── App.vue                 # root component
│   └── main.ts                 # app entry point
├── .editorconfig               # editor settings
├── .gitattributes              # Git attributes
├── .gitignore                  # Git ignore file
├── .prettierrc.json            # Prettier formatting config
├── env.d.ts                    # TypeScript env type definitions
├── eslint.config.ts            # ESLint config
├── index.html                  # HTML entry file
├── package.json                # project dependencies and scripts
├── playwright.config.ts        # Playwright E2E config
├── README.md                   # project readme
├── tsconfig.app.json           # TypeScript config
├── tsconfig.json               # main TypeScript config
├── tsconfig.node.json          # Node.js TypeScript config
├── tsconfig.vitest.json        # Vitest TypeScript config
├── vite.config.ts              # Vite build tool config
└── vitest.config.ts            # Vitest test config
That's actually a lot of info, so let me simplify it like this:

vue-example/
├── .vscode/                    # VSCode editor settings
├── e2e/                        # E2E tests (Playwright)
├── public/                     # static assets
├── src/                        # source code directory
│   ├── assets/                 # asset files
│   ├── components/             # Vue components
│   ├── router/                 # Vue Router config
│   ├── stores/                 # Pinia state management
│   ├── views/                  # page components
│   ├── App.vue                 # root component
│   └── main.ts                 # app entry point
├── index.html                  # HTML entry file
├── package.json                # project dependencies and scripts
├── README.md                   # project readme
├── vite.config.ts              # Vite build tool config
└── vitest.config.ts            # Vitest test config
These folders and files are what you'll deal with most, especially when you're starting out. So here are the main Vue project structure patterns.
This one is great for small projects and it's the Vue project structure I just showed above. Basically you put everything under
— components, router, state management, page components, you name it. The upside is you can find what you need quickly.

But this structure does have some issues. As the project grows it gets messy — you won't know which folder a component belongs in, separation of concerns gets blurry, and the worst part is extending the project becomes really hard because you don't know where to start.
So once a flat-structure project grows past a certain point, you usually have to restructure it. I'd generally recommend Flat Structure for

If you're a frontend dev, I'm sure the word "Atomic" isn't new to you — Tailwind CSS is built on the atomic concept after all. Atomic Structure follows the same idea: break the project into smaller units that can each run independently. The upside is the project gets easier to maintain because each unit is self-contained, and you can find what you need more easily.
Atomic has a few key levels though:
Atoms: the smallest unit — buttons, input fields, headings. Good for single-purpose components.
Molecules: units made of multiple atoms — search bars, forms, etc.
Organisms: units made of multiple molecules — navbars, footers, etc.
Templates: units made of multiple organisms — page layouts, etc.
Pages: the final pages, containing all templates and content.
You might've noticed something — if we use Tailwind CSS as the analogy, you go from the smallest class up to larger components, and finally to a page. The table below maps out the relationship:
Smallest unit, can't be broken down further
Single-purpose component made of multiple atoms
Complex section made of molecules and atoms
Page layout made of multiple organisms
Final page filled with real content
So what does a Vue project structure based on Atomic look like? Here it is:

vue-example/
├── .vscode/                    # VSCode editor settings
├── e2e/                        # E2E tests (Playwright)
├── public/                     # static assets
├── src/                        # source code directory
│   ├── assets/                 # asset files
│   ├── components/             # Vue components
│   │   ├── atoms/              # atom components
│   │   ├── molecules/          # molecule components
│   │   ├── organisms/          # organism components
│   │   └── templates/          # template components
│   ├── pages/                  # page components
│   ├── router/                 # Vue Router config
│   ├── stores/                 # Pinia state management
│   ├── App.vue                 # root component
│   └── main.ts                 # app entry point
├── index.html                  # HTML entry file
├── package.json                # project dependencies and scripts
├── README.md                   # project readme
├── vite.config.ts              # Vite build tool config
└── vitest.config.ts            # Vitest test config
. This gives you better separation of concerns since each layer is clearly split.
If you're not sure how to combine them, here's an example of building Pages from Atoms:

Pages
└── HomePage.vue
    ├── Main.vue (Template)
    │   ├── Header.vue (Organism)
    │   │   ├── Logo.vue (Atom)
    │   │   └── NavBar.vue (Molecule)
    │   │       ├── NavItem.vue (Atom)
    │   │       └── Dropdown.vue (Molecule)
    │   ├── Content.vue (Organism)
    │   │   ├── ArticleList.vue (Molecule)
    │   │   │   ├── ArticleItem.vue (Atom)
    │   │   │   └── ReadMoreButton.vue (Atom)
    │   │   └── Sidebar.vue (Molecule)
    │   │       ├── SearchBox.vue (Atom)
    │   │       └── CategoryList.vue (Molecule)
    │   │           └── CategoryItem.vue (Atom)
    │   └── Footer.vue (Organism)
    │       ├── FooterLink.vue (Atom)
    │       └── SocialMediaIcons.vue (Molecule)
    │           └── SocialMediaIcon.vue (Atom)

You'll see this is super extensible, but managing the layers gets more complex, so the upfront planning is heavier. That said, this structure is great for medium-to-large projects, and especially good for building a UI Framework — UI Frameworks usually need a ton of atoms, which then get combined into molecules, and so on.
That's also why UI Frameworks typically expose all the layers of Atomic Structure to their users.

As the name suggests, the core idea of Modular Structure is that each module is its own independent unit. If module A needs something from module B, you don't drop B's stuff under A — you import it instead. This drastically reduces coupling between modules and lets each module run on its own, which is also good prep for a microservices architecture.
The standout part here is the
folder, which holds all the modules. Each module is its own folder containing everything it needs — components, router, state management, the lot.

vue-example/
├── .vscode/                    # VSCode editor settings
├── e2e/                        # E2E tests (Playwright)
├── public/                     # static assets
├── src/                        # source code directory
│   ├── assets/                 # asset files
│   ├── modules/                # modules directory
│   │   ├── moduleA/            # module A
│   │   │   ├── components/     # module A components
│   │   │   ├── router/         # module A routes
│   │   │   │  └── index.ts        # module A routes entry
│   │   │   ├── stores/         # module A state
│   │   │   └── index.ts        # module A entry
│   │   └── moduleB/            # module B
│   │       ├── components/     # module B components
│   │       ├── router/         # module B routes
│   │       │  └── index.ts        # module B routes entry
│   │       ├── stores/         # module B state
│   │       └── index.ts        # module B entry
│   ├── router/                 # global router config
│   ├── stores/                 # global state
│   ├── App.vue                 # root component
│   └── main.ts                 # app entry point
├── index.html                  # HTML entry file
├── package.json                # project dependencies and scripts
├── README.md                   # project readme
├── vite.config.ts              # Vite build tool config
└── vitest.config.ts            # Vitest test config
At this point you might wonder — how do the routes inside
integrate with the global router? We can use dynamic imports, like this:
only needs to define that module's own routes:
Here's the thing you might notice — when another project needs the same module, you just copy the whole
folder over. No need to worry about other modules interfering.
The biggest issue with Modular Structure is deep nesting. When modules pile up, the project structure gets really complex, and you'll need solid naming conventions to keep things readable.
I didn't really cover Nuxt's project structure here because Nuxt comes with its own —
, and so on. Nuxt has all of that figured out for you.
On top of that, Nuxt has Auto Import, so you don't have to manually import components, composables, or plugins — that cuts down a lot of project-structure overhead.
And of course you can use Nuxt's
feature to get a modular structure in your Nuxt project too.

At the end of the day, whichever structure you pick, the most important thing is that it fits your project needs — project size, team size, dev workflow, and so on. Don't just use a structure because you like it. If your team isn't familiar with Modular Structure, forcing it will only make the project more complex.

And these structures aren't absolute either. You can adjust them to fit your needs — combine Atomic and Modular, split folders by feature, or come up with your own variation that fits your team. All of those are totally fine.
